headline: Checking in on the UK’s gilt-ridden finances
summary: Barclays and the IFS on what the UK needs to do to avoid a funding crisis
body_text:
We were all ready to write a post on how bad gilts — and government bonds more generally — have been this year. But:

Sure, bonds have lagged stocks, crypto and gold. But aside from JGBs, returns on 7-10 year G7 government bonds are all in the black. And on a total return basis gilts have been outshone only by US Treasuries.

However, investors being rewarded for holding gilts is different from gilt yields behaving as HM Treasury would like. Yields have stayed belligerently high despite Bank of England rate cuts and the rally in US Treasuries, to which they’ve often been joined at the hip. They are now the highest in the G7:

This is despite many so-called ‘fundamentals’ — those things supposedly connecting securities pricing to macroeconomic performance — being comparatively less than terrible in the UK than they are across the rest of the G7. The IMF projects UK deficits to shrink rapidly, and overall debt to stabilise — in marked contrast to many peers.

So why then have gilt yields
